desc: Largest LA robber fly. Catches prey in mid-air including dragonflies and grasshoppers. Bristly, powerful.

fm.Behavior: Aerial predator; catches prey in flight; perches on prominent twigs
fm.Diet: Predator — dragonflies, grasshoppers, bees, other large insects

desc: Large robber fly with bright orange-red legs. Named for eating other robber flies. Impressive size.

fm.Behavior: Aggressive aerial predator; sits on fence posts, bare branches
fm.Diet: Predator — other robber flies, bees, large insects

desc: Fuzzy hoverfly-like fly that feeds at flowers while hovering. Long proboscis. Larvae parasitize solitary bees.

fm.Color: Fuzzy golden-brown; long straight proboscis; dark wing front edge
fm.Behavior: Hovers at flowers; flicks eggs into bee burrows in flight
fm.Habitat: Gardens, chaparral, meadows — near ground-nesting bees
fm.Diet: Adults: nectar. Larvae: parasitize ground-nesting bee larvae

desc: Large, spectacular bee fly with mottled tiger-pattern wings. Parasitizes carpenter bees.

fm.Behavior: Hovers near carpenter bee nests; lays eggs in nest holes
fm.Habitat: Near wooden structures with carpenter bee holes
fm.Diet: Adults: nectar. Larvae: parasitize carpenter bee larvae

desc: Small bee fly with strikingly patterned black-and-clear wings. Common on flowers in chaparral.

fm.Color: Black body; wings with bold black pattern on clear ground
fm.Behavior: Visits flowers; parasitizes other fly larvae
fm.Diet: Adults: nectar. Larvae: hyperparasitoids

desc: Largest NA fly — wingspan can exceed 3 in. Harmless despite mosquito-like appearance. Cannot bite.

fm.Color: Brown; very long legs; mosquito-like but MUCH larger
fm.Behavior: Attracted to lights; clumsy flight; adults do not feed; completely harmless
fm.Habitat: Near streams, moist areas — adults at lights
fm.Diet: Adults: do not feed (mouthparts non-functional). Larvae: aquatic detritus

desc: Large biting fly. Females require blood meal for eggs. Painful slashing bite. Found near livestock and water.

fm.Behavior: Females bite — slash skin, lap blood; painful; males visit flowers
fm.Diet: Females: blood. Males: nectar, pollen

desc: Bright orange abdomen; distinctive feathered hind legs. Parasitizes stink bugs and squash bugs. Beneficial.

fm.Color: Black thorax; bright orange abdomen; feathered hind tibiae
fm.Behavior: Parasitizes squash bugs and stink bugs; lays eggs on host
fm.Diet: Adults: nectar. Larvae: parasitize Hemiptera (beneficial)

desc: Metallic green-gold. First insect to colonize carrion — important in forensic entomology. Larvae used medically for wound cleaning.

fm.Behavior: First to arrive at carrion; important forensic indicator
fm.Habitat: Ubiquitous — attracted to carrion, garbage, wounds
fm.Diet: Larvae: carrion, decaying tissue

desc: Metallic blue. Large, buzzy. Common around garbage and pet waste. Distinctive loud buzzing.

fm.Behavior: Loud buzzer; attracted to meat, garbage
fm.Diet: Larvae: carrion, waste

desc: Looks like a house fly but BITES. Forward-pointing proboscis. Bites legs of humans and livestock.

fm.Color: Gray; like house fly but with bayonet-like proboscis (forward-pointing)
fm.Behavior: Painful biter; bites legs; rests on walls with head up; blood feeder
fm.Habitat: Near livestock, stables, dog runs — organic waste
fm.Diet: Blood — mammals including humans

desc: Floats through shaded woodland like a phantom. Inflated tarsi act as parachutes. Ethereal slow flight.

fm.Size: Body ⅓ in–½ in; legs very long with inflated tarsi
fm.Color: Black and white banded legs; inflated white tarsal segments
fm.Behavior: Drifts slowly through shade; inflated feet act as parachutes; ethereal
fm.Diet: Adults: do not feed. Larvae: aquatic

desc: One of the largest flies — wasp mimic with orange abdominal band. Impressive hovering. Larvae in rotting wood.

fm.Color: Black with orange second abdominal segment — wasp mimic
fm.Behavior: Powerful hoverer; visits flowers; Batesian wasp mimic
fm.Diet: Adults: nectar. Larvae: in rotting wood/soil (predatory)

desc: Black soldier fly. Larvae are champion composters — process organic waste rapidly. Commercially raised for waste management and animal feed.

fm.Color: Black; wasp-like; two translucent 'windows' on abdomen
fm.Behavior: Adults harmless; larvae consume organic waste; commercial composting
fm.Diet: Larvae: decomposing organic matter (champion composters)

desc: Dreaded invasive fruit pest. Periodic outbreaks trigger quarantine zones. NOT the tiny Drosophila vinegar fly.

fm.Color: Yellow-brown; patterned wings; large for a fruit fly
fm.Behavior: Lays eggs in ripening fruit; causes massive agricultural damage
fm.Diet: Larvae: ripening fruit — citrus, stone fruit, many others

desc: Invasive from Asia. Unlike other vinegar flies, attacks RIPE (not rotting) fruit. Males have dark wing spots. Major berry pest.

fm.Color: Tan; ♂ has dark spot on each wing (diagnostic)
fm.Behavior: Lays eggs in intact ripe fruit; devastating berry/cherry pest
fm.Habitat: Berry farms, orchards, gardens with fruit
fm.Diet: Larvae: ripe fruit — berries, cherries, grapes

desc: Giant, iridescent mosquito that does NOT bite humans. Larvae are predatory — eat other mosquito larvae. Beneficial.

fm.Size: ½ in–⅔ in (giant for a mosquito)
fm.Color: Metallic blue-green iridescence; curved proboscis (can't pierce skin)
fm.Behavior: Hovers near vegetation; does NOT bite; larvae eat mosquito larvae
fm.Habitat: Tree holes, containers — wherever standing water collects
fm.Diet: Adults: nectar only. Larvae: predatory on mosquito larvae (beneficial)

desc: Gray, sluggish fly that clusters in buildings fall/winter. Larvae parasitize earthworms. Not a filth fly.

fm.Color: Dark gray; golden hairs on thorax; sluggish
fm.Behavior: Mass clusters in buildings fall/winter; sluggish; not attracted to garbage
fm.Diet: Adults: nectar. Larvae: parasitize earthworms

desc: Attractive small fly with banded/patterned wings. Waves wings in display. On decaying vegetation.

fm.Color: Dark with patterned/banded wings; wing-waving behavior
fm.Behavior: Elaborate wing-waving display; on decaying plants
fm.Diet: Larvae: decaying plant material

desc: Small gray fly whose larvae are the 'root maggots' that damage Brassicaceae crops. Important agricultural pest.

fm.Color: Gray; similar to small house fly
fm.Behavior: Lays eggs at base of plants; larvae bore into roots
fm.Diet: Larvae: roots of Brassicaceae (major pest)

desc: Tiny, fuzzy, moth-like fly found in bathrooms. Breeds in drain biofilm. Also called drain fly. Harmless.

fm.Behavior: Sits on bathroom walls; breeds in drain biofilm; poor flier
fm.Diet: Larvae: biofilm in drains and sewage

desc: Tiny wingless fly that rides on honeybees. Not a true louse. Steals food from bee's mouth. Declining with bee declines.

fm.Color: Brown; round; wingless; resembles a mite
fm.Behavior: Rides on adult honeybees; steals food from bee's mouth (kleptoparasite)
fm.Diet: Kleptoparasite — steals food from honeybees

desc: Metallic green fly. Forensically important — one of first insects to colonize remains. Also causes myiasis in sheep.
Tiny biting midge causing seasonal misery in inland valleys. Breeds in moist soil. Worse at dawn/dusk.

fm.Size: Tiny biting midge causing seasonal misery in inland valleys

desc: Tiny fly that runs rapidly rather than flying. Breeds in decaying organic matter. Forensic indicator species.

fm.Size: Tiny fly that runs rapidly rather than flying

desc: Flat wingless fly parasitizing pigeons. Blood-feeder. Also called pigeon fly. Vectors avian malaria.
Tiny fuzzy fly breeding in sink and shower drains. Indicator of biofilm buildup. Harmless.

fm.Size: Tiny fuzzy fly breeding in sink and shower drains

desc: Small dark fly on windows. Larvae in fermenting organic matter. Sometimes called wood gnats.
Large bristly-faced robber fly ambushing other insects in flight. Powerful predator of bees and wasps.

fm.Prey: Bees, wasps, other flies — caught in flight

desc: Banded-wing biting fly. Females need blood for eggs. Iridescent green eyes. Near water.
Large bristly fly parasitizing caterpillars. Important natural biocontrol. Larvae develop inside host.
Gray-striped fly depositing live larvae on carrion. Forensically important. Three dark thoracic stripes.

fm.Color: Gray with three dark thoracic stripes
fm.Habitat: Carrion; also parasitic on other insects
fm.Behavior: Deposits live larvae not eggs (larviparous)

desc: Tiny metallic green fly on leaves. Males wave ornate leg flags in courtship. Predatory on small insects.

fm.Behavior: ♂ waves ornate leg flags in courtship display

desc: Small black fly found indoors on windows. Larvae predatory on carpet beetle larvae in carpets.

fm.Habitat: Small black fly found indoors on windows

desc: Predatory fly perching head-down on tree trunks. Pounces on passing insects. Pointed head.

fm.Size: Predatory fly perching head-down on tree trunks

desc: Small fly breeding in rotting kelp on beaches. Important decomposer. Huge swarms on wrack line.

fm.Habitat: Small fly breeding in rotting kelp on beaches

desc: Black fly swarming in spring. Males with huge eyes. Larvae feed on roots and decaying matter in soil.
Wasp-mimicking fly. Internal parasitoid of bumble bees — larva develops inside living bee.
World's most widespread fly. Pest and disease vector. Breeds in organic waste. Complex eyes with 4000 facets.

fm.Color: Gray with four dark thoracic stripes

desc: Tiny vinegar fly on ripening fruit. Most studied animal in genetics — Nobel Prizes won using this species.

fm.Role: Most important model organism in genetics

desc: One of the largest flies in NA. Ambushes large insects including dragonflies and bees. Powerful predator.
Tiny fly parasitizing fire ants. Lays egg in ant — larva decapitates host. Released as biocontrol of fire ants.
Large dark fly. Larvae develop as subcutaneous parasites in rodents and rabbits. Occasionally infests pets.

fm.Role: Parasitoid — larvae develop under host skin

desc: Small fly with banded wings. Larvae bore into cherries. Important stone fruit pest in West.
Small fly with banded wings. Larvae stain and damage walnut husks. Common wherever walnuts grow.
Fruit fly with distinctive wing pattern. Quarantine pest — not established in CA. Active detection program.

fm.Habitat: Apple orchards — quarantine pest in CA

desc: Devastating olive pest from Mediterranean. Arrived in CA 1998. Larvae tunnel through olive flesh.

fm.Habitat: Olive trees — established in CA since 1998

desc: Invasive ankle-biter established in LA County since 2011. Day-biting. Vectors dengue, Zika, chikungunya — first locally acquired dengue in LA history 2024.

fm.Size: ¼ in; black with white leg bands
fm.Habitat: Urban — breeds in any tiny water container
fm.Behavior: Aggressive daytime biter
fm.Disease: Vectors dengue, Zika, chikungunya, yellow fever

desc: Invasive black-and-white striped mosquito. Aggressive daytime biter. Established in parts of LA County. Vectors dengue and chikungunya.

fm.Size: ¼ in; black with single white dorsal stripe
fm.Habitat: Urban — any small container with standing water

desc: LA's most common native mosquito. Night-biting. Primary vector of West Nile virus in Southern California.

fm.Habitat: Storm drains, pools, any stagnant water
fm.Disease: Primary West Nile virus vector in SoCal

desc: Invasive Old World blow fly — most abundant blow fly in LA County. Metallic blue-green. Predatory larvae. Key forensic entomology species.
Invasive Old World blow fly. Metallic blue-green with dark anterior spiracle. Forensically important.
Tiny humpbacked biting fly breeding in flowing water. Target of LA River vector control programs. Females slash skin.

fm.Behavior: Females bite painfully; huge swarms near water

desc: Tiny fly whose larvae mine serpentine trails through leaves. Major greenhouse and agricultural pest.

fm.Damage: Larvae mine visible serpentine trails in leaves

desc: Small dark fly breeding in decomposing kelp on LA beaches. Essential coastal decomposer.

fm.Habitat: Sandy beaches — decomposing kelp wrack line

desc: The 'mosquito-like' insect that doesn't bite. Enormous swarms near water at dusk. Males have feathery antennae. Larvae ('bloodworms') are bright red.

fm.Behavior: Huge dusk swarms; does NOT bite

desc: The 'giant mosquito' that isn't — completely harmless. Introduced. Larvae ('leatherjackets') damage turf.

fm.Size: ½–⅔ in body; very long legs
fm.Behavior: Cannot bite or sting; clumsy flier

desc: The small fly that hovers in lazy triangular patterns under ceiling lights. Smaller and more slender than house fly.

fm.Size: ⅛–¼ in; smaller than house fly
fm.Behavior: Males hover in lazy triangles under lights

desc: Tiny dark fly swarming around houseplant soil. Larvae feed on fungi and roots in potting mix. #1 indoor plant pest fly.
Tiny fly that induces distinctive round bud galls on coyote bush. Galls far more conspicuous than the 1mm adults.

fm.Gall: Round bud galls on Baccharis — very conspicuous

desc: Large gray fly with three black thoracic stripes and checkered abdomen. Deposits live larvae (not eggs) on carrion and excrement. Common urban fly. Forensic indicator.

fm.Color: Gray with 3 black thoracic stripes; checkered abdomen
fm.Behavior: Deposits live larvae (larviparity)

desc: Slender tachinid with orange-red head and narrow waist. Parasitizes true bugs (Pentatomidae). A beneficial insect that helps control stink bugs.

fm.Color: Orange-red head; black body; narrow waist

desc: Fuzzy bee mimic with long fixed proboscis. Hovers at flowers. Larvae are parasitoids of solitary bee nests. Flicks eggs into bee nest entrances while hovering.

fm.Behavior: Flicks eggs into solitary bee nests while hovering

desc: Large flat wasp-mimic fly with yellow abdominal markings. Larvae aquatic in stagnant water. Adults visit flowers. Important pollinator.

fm.Color: Black with yellow abdominal spots; flat body
fm.Habitat: Near water — larvae aquatic; adults on flowers

desc: Tiny brilliant metallic green fly on leaf surfaces. Males perform elaborate courtship displays waving modified tarsi. Predatory on tiny insects. Very common in gardens.

fm.Habitat: Leaf surfaces in gardens — tiny predator
fm.Behavior: ♂ elaborate courtship with modified feet

desc: Wasp-mimic fly with enlarged head. Parasitoid — female intercepts bumblebees in flight, inserts egg into abdomen. Larva develops inside living bee. Remarkable biology.

fm.Color: Black and yellow wasp mimic; enlarged head
fm.Behavior: Parasitizes living bumblebees — injects egg in flight

desc: Similar to house fly but eyes closer together (♀) or touching (♂). Feeds on eye and nose secretions of livestock. Enters houses in fall seeking overwintering sites.

fm.Color: Gray; eyes closer together than house fly
fm.Habitat: Near livestock — feeds on facial secretions
fm.Behavior: Invades homes in fall for winter shelter

desc: Small fly with striking dark-patterned wings. Waves wings in semaphore-like courtship display on leaves. Breeds in decaying vegetation. Common on compost.

fm.Behavior: Waves wings in semaphore display on leaf surfaces

desc: Small dark fly of alkaline lakes and saline pools. Larvae feed on algae in mineral-rich water. Important food source for shorebirds at Mono Lake (and similar LA County playas).
Tiny fly attracted to eyes and wounds. Persistent and annoying. Can transmit pinkeye. The classic 'annoying SoCal gnat.'
